text:
My old man's in trouble. Who's gonna save us?

Not Hollywood, maybe someone from the White House this time.

#oott https://t.co/SanQQREqsS

quotedTweet.text:
The Vin Diesel of Oil

You read that right... me and Vin Diesel, we got a lot in common. Bit on the heftier side and all our muscles are there to keep our industries going. My yields can be anywhere from 15-30% of my old man, Crude and also depending on our factory called Refinery.

Just like Vin, I'm big on family. Sister Jet is closest to me and we form the Middle Distillates gang. Brother Fuel Oil is heavier than me and he's the reliable guy. 

When Jet's in trouble, I'm there to give her some of me. 

When Fuel Oil's in trouble, I'm there to blend down his sulphur.

When I'm in trouble, Jet starves a little to give me a little bit of her and Fuel Oil cranks up the heat at the Refinery to produce more of me.

But when old man Crude and our Refinery is in trouble - well, there's not much saving us.

Our Refinery in Russia is being attacked, so can't make much more of me. But my popularity remains in Europe and USA as well. Gotta produce more of me to keep up with demand! 

The world is waiting on China to help, but I'm afraid they are an estranged family, who just wants to maintain order in their own backyard in the midst of this US-Iran war. 

Someone's gotta pay to keep demand for me high even when supply is getting short. Here's my namecard.

#oott

text: With electricity demand growing so fast, even the spectacular growth in PV (and wind, to a lesser extent) is insufficient to meet the annual increase in consumption, let alone make a dent on baseload fossil fuel output. As a result, coal is still called on to fill the gap.

U.S. OIL REFINERS are running flat out to meet the global demand for fuel as war disrupts supplies from the Persian Gulf and Russia. U.S. refiners have processed an average of 17.3 million barrels of crude and other feedstocks so far this month, utilising 96% of their total operable capacity of 18.0 million b/d. Processing has been the fastest for the time of year since 17.6 million b/d in 2019, when refiners were utilising 94% of their much higher operable capacity of 18.8 million b/d. But maximising utilisation to satisfy demand leaves no contingency to deal with accidents or any other form of disruption and limited capacity to rebuild depleted inventories – which is why crack spreads and refining margins have been trading at or round record highs:

NYT:

Iran on Thursday rejected a cease-fire proposal from President Trump that was carried to Tehran by the Iraqi prime minister, according to Iranian and Iraqi officials. The failed mediation effort came as Mr. Trump was threatening to escalate the war and target Iran’s critical infrastructure.

The officials, who asked not to be identified because they were discussing sensitive national security issues, said Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi’s trip to Iran followed his recent visit with Mr. Trump at the White House.

The details of the cease-fire proposal were not immediately clear. But Iranian officials said that it was the only offer on the table, and that their government was not interested in a temporary deal that left the question of control over the Strait of Hormuz unresolved.

Mr. al-Zaidi traveled to Tehran with a delegation of senior Iraqi officials and met with President Masoud Pezeshkian; Iran’s top negotiator, Brig. Gen. Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf; and its foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, according to local media reports.

Mr. Araghchi told state television that Iran and Iraq, which share a border, had mutual economic, political and security interests and close ties and that the visit was significant given the current circumstances in the region.

Mr. Araghchi said that Mr. al-Zaidi had shared his “views and impressions” from his trip to Washington, but that Tehran and Washington already had enough mediators. “The problem is not passing messages,” he said. “The problem is America’s outlook, which is illogical, greedy and controlling.”
Iran and the United States have steadily escalated tensions over the past few weeks, with American forces bombing military bases, as well as infrastructure like railroads, airports, bridges and seaports. Iran has launched strikes with ballistic missiles and drones on American military targets in the Middle East.

Two Iranian officials said that the country’s armed forces were anticipating — and preparing for — the possibility of the war expanding if Mr. Trump carries through with his threats to strike the capital of Tehran and critical infrastructure. If he does, the officials said, Iran will expand the fight regionally, including by striking Tel Aviv and asking its Houthi allies to close Bab al-Mandab, a strategic waterway in the Red Sea.

On Thursday in Tehran’s Azadi Square, where supporters of the government stage nightly pro-war rallies, a military vehicle paraded Iranian-made ballistic missiles pointing at the sky.

The Only Way Out Is Through

The oil math is about to get really bad.

https://t.co/2jrThejADm

Even with the surge in crude, refining margins aren't falling that much because global product storage is low.

As a result, product prices will squeeze higher with crude, creating a vicious cycle. https://t.co/qdQTi6jDio

From the public article:

Variant Perception

It’s hard to believe that Brent is trading at $77/bbl, IRGC controls the Strait of Hormuz, 7 to 8 million b/d of crude oil production remains shut-in, and the market is max short Brent.

And the guy who’s long oil (me) is making a contrarian bet.

Unprecedented.

I don’t know whether I am currently stuck in the Matrix and the system is just messing with me, but this is the most surreal moment of my life so far. Either I am the stupidest person on the planet, or everyone has lost their mind. I think my view on the market is also about as binary as the setup I am seeing.

JPM’s EM analyst still wins the most ridiculous take of the decade with his 200% pre conflict vessel traffic analysis back in early June.

quotedTweet.text:
Oh my god, this is so good.

This is JPM EM's method for counting vessels that contributed to its claim of "200% of pre-conflict" levels.

Method: Take snapshots of AIS data for vessels on each side of the Strait via Bloomberg's MAP function once a day. Identify confirmed transits (vessels appearing on one side of the strait and then subsequently on the other), and exclude support vessels (e.g. tug, landing craft, platform supply). In this methodology, we may on occasions miss some shadow vessels (if they re-appear only days later or far away), but we are able to capture vast majority of shadow crossings that have been reported in other media.

Average taken over weekends.

text:
Insanity. 

FT:

Staying close to the Omani coast, the vessels — Kavomaleas and Acheloos — attempted the crossing on Sunday night, the only two ships to do so, according to people with knowledge of the situation.

Despite reinforced US air support and days of heavy attacks on military targets along the coast, both were hit and damaged by Iranian missiles.

“Hitting the Dynacom vessels was a game-changer, and even the vessel owners who are prepared to take risks will need more than headlines about peace to come back in,” said Amrita Sen, founder of consultancy Energy Aspects.

Military briefing: can the US reopen the Strait of Hormuz by force? 

https://t.co/pMT1ZC8ac2

Production shut-in math update 7/22 (in million b/d):

Saudi - 3.5

Iraq - 3

UAE - 0.5

Kuwait - 1.8

Bahrain - 0.17

Qatar - 1.1

Iran - 1.1

Total: 11.17 million b/d

Strait of Hormuz over the last 24 hours: Oman lane is dead.

Visible traffic continues to be nonexistent in the Oman lane, but the Iranians reported that 3 vessels attempted to transit with US escorts only to have one get attacked. The other 2 turned around. 

With the Bab al-Mandeb closure now in force by the Houthis, Fujairah enforcement is the most likely next escalation path. 

Despite tankers being attacked during their transits through the Oman lane, some brave shipowners and captains are still trying, which means the enforcement has to go to the root cause: Fujairah.

I guess we will see where this is all going, but from an oil math standpoint, this is the endgame now.

Video is from @MarineTraffic

Escalation ladder on the oil math:

- Oman lane: 6 million b/d, 2 million b/d UAE, 4 million b/d Saudi/Kuwait/Iraq

- Bab al-Mandeb: 5 million b/d, 4 in crude, 1 in product. Bypass through Suez, loading speed drops (1.5 million b/d)

- Fujairah: 2 million b/d, zero ship to ship

Oman lane and BAM already in play, 7.5 million b/d of crude offline. 

Fujairah is the last card. If that’s played, it’s game over. 

No idea why tanker owners still risk going there. It’s like shooting fish in a barrel. Terrible risk to reward ratio.
JPM, Goldman, Citi, and Morgan Stanley have been awfully quiet as of late.

The head of the International Maritime Organization (IMO) condemned the latest attacks in the Red Sea, saying they threaten seafarers, international shipping, global supply chains and the marine environment.

"The Red Sea is a critical maritime corridor for international trade. Continued attacks on shipping in this region risk escalating tensions, further disrupting commercial routes and undermining the principle of freedom of navigation," IMO Secretary-General Arsenio Dominguez said in a statement.

"De-escalation is the only solution."

He added that he is "equally concerned" about the potential for pollution from reported attacks in the area and the Strait of Hormuz.

"IMO will continue to monitor the situation and stands ready to assist as necessary.” https://t.co/tNahLEv1UZ

The CPC oil terminal is still presumed to be offline.

Same as two days back—both outer SPMs are still sitting bone dry, with only the center SPM holding what looks like the exact same vessel. 

We are now officially on day 5 with zero tanker traffic showing up at SPM-2 and 3.

Another interesting note: there isn't a single berthed tanker visible at the Sheskharis terminal in the Novorossiysk either.

Safe to say Putin’s mood isn't exactly shining right now.

#oott #russia

quotedTweet.text:
Fresh optical satellite print of the CPC terminal from 5 hours ago. And i was running SAR satellite passes on it all weekend. 

We got lucky with back to back orbits over the CPC terminal across the 18th, 19th, and 20th.

Both outer SPMs have been sitting totally bone dry since at least the 19th, with a lone vessel berthed at the center SPM. Still no confirmation on whether that vessel is taking on barrels or just parking there.

What's locked in, though, is that two SPMs have printed zero tanker traffic for three days straight. Safe to say at least two of those SPMs are knocked out of service.

#oott #russia
